description: Adding currency dimension and rebuilding the materialized view that powers the merchant analytics dashboard. Review concurrency, dependent objects, and the refresh strategy.
review_checklist: Identify ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT semantics on PG 11+
Verify dependent objects survive view rebuild
Distinguish REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW vs CONCURRENTLY
Audit cache freshness model and refresh cadence

ground_truth.description: ADD COLUMN currency CHAR(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'USD' on transactions (1.5B rows). PG 11+ supports this as metadata-only WHEN the default is non-volatile and IMMUTABLE. 'USD' literal qualifies, so this is fast — but ONLY if the default is set in the same statement. Current statement is correct, but a common bug is to ADD COLUMN, then ALTER...SET DEFAULT separately, which forces a rewrite. Document the requirement.

DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW merchant_daily_summary will fail or cascade-break dependent objects. Per the schema comments, this view is consumed by 3 production services. The DROP itself takes AccessExclusiveLock on the view; any indexes on the view are also dropped. Use CREATE OR REPLACE pattern (PG 14+ for matviews) or do a swap: create new view with different name, atomically rename.

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW (without CONCURRENTLY) acquires AccessExclusiveLock on the view, making it unreadable for the entire refresh duration (~15 min on 90 days × 1.5B-row source). All 3 dependent services see hard errors during this window. Use REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY (requires UNIQUE INDEX on the view).

New matview definition lacks a UNIQUE INDEX, which means future REFRESH cannot use CONCURRENTLY mode. Always add `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON merchant_daily_summary(merchant_id, currency, day)` after view creation; this enables non-blocking refresh going forward.

Architectural smell: matview has 90-day window via `WHERE occurred_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'`. NOW() is captured at view DEFINITION time but refreshes use the same query — meaning each REFRESH re-evaluates the window. This is correct, but expensive: every refresh scans 90 days of transactions (~250M rows) even though only the last day changed. Consider incremental/delta materialization via pg_ivm or a partitioned daily summary table built from a continuous aggregate (TimescaleDB) or scheduled deltas.

currency CHAR(3) is a poor data type choice. CHAR(3) pads to fixed width with spaces — 'USD' stored, 'USD ' compared, leading to subtle bugs. Use VARCHAR(3) or even better, ENUM/lookup table referencing ISO 4217 currency codes.
